metadata.match_reason: Series A Feb 2026 ($15M, Elevation+Lightspeed); LLM gateway, observability,  prompt mgmt, RAG playground â€” Python/FastAPI/vector DB stack exactly Amit's profile. ~45 employees, high-impact hire.
metadata.notes: AI Gateway processes 250B+ tokens.  Cold email: '2 yrs RAG/FastAPI shipping production LLM apps.'

research_summary: Portkey AI (~45 employees) builds an AI Gateway + LLMOps control plane processing 14B tokens/day across 24K customers. Raised $15M Series A (Feb 2026, Elevation + Lightspeed), then announced acquisition by Palo Alto Networks (Apr 30, 2026, close expected Q4 FY2026). Founders: Rohit Agarwal (CEO, @jumbld) and Ayush Garg (CTO). Engineering team in Bengaluru. Email pattern firstname@portkey.ai confirmed. Key recent signals: Portkey CLI launch (May 2026), Ayush's 'agent control is a gateway problem' thesis, acquisition announcement. Hiring may have slowed post-acquisition -- user should verify before sending.
jd_full_text:
Software Engineer (AI/Backend, LLMOps) -- Portkey, Bengaluru

Portkey is building a full-stack LLMOps platform that empowers AI builders to productionize their Gen AI apps reliably and securely.

Role: Software Engineer
Location: Bengaluru, India
Experience: 3-5 years backend development
Salary: 25-40 LPA

Requirements (extracted from Instahyre listing and platform context):
- 3-5 years of backend development experience
- Proficiency in Python, Node.js
- Proven experience in a startup environment, showcasing adaptability and agility
- Experience building LLMOps / AI infrastructure: gateway routing, observability, prompt management, guardrails
- Comfort working across providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, open-source LLMs)
- Production-grade systems thinking: reliability, latency, cost optimization across LLM calls

Platform context:
- AI Gateway: routes LLM calls across 250+ providers with fallbacks, load balancing, caching
- Observability: real-time monitoring, logging, analytics for LLM usage
- Prompt management: versioning, A/B testing, deployment pipelines
- Guardrails and governance: content filtering, PII detection, policy enforcement
- RAG playground and evaluation frameworks

(Note: full JD text unavailable -- Instahyre required login. Reconstructed from Google snippet and Portkey public product documentation.)
tailoring_notes: Summary rewritten to lead with production LLM infrastructure and multi-provider routing -- JD is a full-stack LLMOps platform, gateway routing, observability. Skills reordered: LLMs and Techniques first (multi-model, LLM Orchestration per JD), Languages 2nd, Vector DBs 3rd, Infrastructure 4th; Hardware/IoT dropped. IIT Delhi trimmed to 1 bullet. Projects: WeepScope replaced with SAP Analytics Bot.

contacts.linkedin: https://www.linkedin.com/in/ (search: Rohit Agarwal Portkey, BITS Pilani, 20.6K followers)
contacts.research_hook: Posted April 30 about the PANW acquisition ('three years after founding' framing, 1.9K reactions). 2 weeks ago launched the Portkey CLI ('npx portkey setup'). 11 months ago wrote 'Why semantic caching is underused at Portkey' -- technical depth on caching LLM responses, directly relevant to Amit's latency work (16s to 8s reduction at Silver Touch).
contacts.subject: went through your gateway docs after the CLI launch
contacts.content:
Hi Rohit,

Went through the Portkey gateway docs after the CLI launch two weeks ago. Your semantic caching post from last year landed for me. We hit a similar latency wall at Silver Touch before moving from FAISS to Milvus, and the bottleneck turned out to be retrieval, not the LLM call. Caching at the right layer would have helped.

Quick context: I'm 2 years into production AI/ML. RAG at 95% MRR across 50K queries/month, multi-model orchestration with GPT-4o, Qwen3-Coder, and GPT-4o-mini in one flow, and a VLLM model gateway at Silver Touch that decoupled the LLM from application code so we could swap providers without a redeploy. The VLLM work is the closest thing I've built to what Portkey does at scale.

If there's an open backend or LLMOps role, I'd love 15 minutes. Totally understand if the PANW timeline makes timing complicated right now.

Amit
amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai | amitgohel.dev/chat
contacts.follow_up_1_subject: re: went through your gateway docs after the CLI launch
contacts.follow_up_1_content:
Hi Rohit,

Bumping this up in case it got buried. One more thought: the PANW announcement framed Portkey's value as 'AI agents are now privileged insiders.' That maps directly to the hardest problem I've worked on. I built a RAG system where 250+ permissions had to be enforced per query and caching was impossible because two users asking the same question legitimately get different answers. A control plane at the gateway layer would have simplified that significantly.

If now isn't the right window, happy to check back in a few weeks.

Amit
amitgohel.dev
contacts.follow_up_2_subject: re: went through your gateway docs after the CLI launch
contacts.follow_up_2_content:
Hi Rohit,

Last note from me on this. If the hiring picture is unclear during the PANW transition, totally understood. Would still appreciate being on your radar when things settle.

Either way, the LLMs in Prod newsletter content has been useful. Good luck with the integration.

Amit
amitgohel.dev

contacts.linkedin: https://www.linkedin.com/in/ (search: Ayush Garg Portkey, Delhi Public School, 10.1K followers)
contacts.research_hook: 3 weeks ago posted 'Agent control is a gateway problem, not an orchestration problem' (60+ reactions) -- argues control (rate limits, fallbacks, guardrails) belongs at the gateway layer. 2 weeks ago posted about npx portkey setup CLI launch. Series A $15M announcement 2 months ago got 480+ reactions.
contacts.subject: your post on gateway control vs orchestration
contacts.content:
Hi Ayush,

Your post three weeks ago about agent control being a gateway problem landed for me. I ran into this building the Form Builder at Vivansh: GPT-4o, Qwen3-Coder-480B, and GPT-4o-mini chained in one flow, and I was writing retry logic at the application layer for each handoff. Wrong place for it. Your point about centralizing control at the gateway is the cleaner design, and I learned it by doing it wrong first.

I'm 2 years into production AI/ML: RAG at 50K queries/month, multi-model orchestration with 3 providers in production, and a VLLM model gateway at Silver Touch that let us swap Llama-2-7b for Nemotron without touching the app. Looking at what's next, and Portkey's control plane work is the closest thing to what I'd want to build.

If you're hiring on the backend or LLMOps side, or there's someone on the eng team I should talk to, I'd appreciate a pointer. Open to a 15-min call next week.

Amit
amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai | amitgohel.dev/chat
contacts.follow_up_1_subject: re: your post on gateway control vs orchestration
contacts.follow_up_1_content:
Hi Ayush,

Following up from last week. One more thing: I shared the Portkey CLI with the team at Vivansh this week as an example of what provider-agnostic developer tooling looks like. The onboarding friction reduction is clear. The part I'm still thinking about is how Portkey handles routing decisions at inference time when latency budgets differ per call type.

Still interested in talking if there's room on the engineering team.

Amit
amitgohel.dev
contacts.follow_up_2_subject: re: your post on gateway control vs orchestration
contacts.follow_up_2_content:
Hi Ayush,

Last note from me. If the team's focus is elsewhere during the PANW integration, completely understood. Happy to circle back when the timing works.

Your 'control plane is no longer optional' framing from the Series A post stuck with me. Either way, appreciate the thinking you share publicly.

Amit
amitgohel.dev

contacts.research_hook: Forward Deployed Engineer focused on agentic AI, LLMOps, and scalable AI systems. IIIT background. Works directly with enterprise customers on LLM production deployments -- exactly the kind of role where Amit's multi-tenant RAG and permission-aware retrieval experience applies.
contacts.subject: question about Portkey's forward deployed engineering
contacts.content:
Hi Krishna,

Came across your profile when looking into Portkey's engineering team. Forward Deployed Engineer focused on agentic AI and LLMOps is a specific niche: working with enterprise customers to debug why their RAG pipeline dropped MRR after a data update, not just shipping features into a void.

That's most of what I've done the past 2 years. At Silver Touch: rebuilt RAG infra serving 50K queries/month, benchmarked 6 embedding models, and ended up at BGE-M3 with a reranker after FAISS at 45% MRR became the bottleneck (Milvus came out at 95%, Qdrant at 85% on the same production data). At Vivansh: permission-aware RAG for 500+ concurrent users (250+ permissions enforced per query, no caching possible) and recently moved the chatbot to a LangGraph-orchestrated agentic architecture with tool calling across 10+ platform APIs.

If Portkey has engineering openings, I'd love 15 minutes. If the timing is off right now, totally understood.

Amit
github.com/amit-gohel | amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai | amitgohel.dev/chat
contacts.follow_up_1_subject: re: question about Portkey's forward deployed engineering
contacts.follow_up_1_content:
Hi Krishna,

Following up from last week. One specific thing I've been curious about: at Silver Touch the hardest part of the RAG deployment was not the retrieval quality. It was understanding why MRR dropped after a routine data update. Turned out to be chunk size drift across batches. That kind of live production debugging seems central to a forward deployed role at an LLMOps company.

If Portkey has open engineering positions, still happy to connect.

Amit
amitgohel.dev
contacts.follow_up_2_subject: re: question about Portkey's forward deployed engineering
contacts.follow_up_2_content:
Hi Krishna,

Last note from me. If the timing isn't right, no problem. Would appreciate being on your radar for future openings.

Amit

contacts.research_hook: Posted 'Making payments conversational with MCP' -- experimenting with Model Context Protocol to expose payment APIs to AI agents. Ex-Samsung R&D intern (Amit: Samsung Solve for Tomorrow Top 10). IIIT Hyderabad, ex-Cashfree Payments. Shows systems integration curiosity beyond core job.
contacts.subject: your MCP payments post and a thought
contacts.content:
Hi Siva,

Saw your post on making payments conversational with MCP. The pattern you described, exposing payment APIs to AI agents via MCP, is similar to something I built at Vivansh: a LangGraph orchestration layer that routes agent tool calls to 10+ platform APIs at runtime. Getting agents to reliably call structured APIs without hallucinating parameters is the hard part in both cases.

Quick context: 2 years of production AI/ML. RAG at 50K queries/month, FAISS to Milvus migration (45% to 95% MRR on production benchmarks, Qdrant came in at 85%), LangChain/LangGraph agentic pipelines, multi-model orchestration in production. Also spent time at Samsung Solve for Tomorrow (Top 10 nationwide out of 70K+ participants, featured on CNN News18), which I mention because of your Samsung R&D background.

If Portkey is hiring on the engineering side, I'd love a quick chat. If not, no worries.

Amit
amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai | github.com/amit-gohel
contacts.follow_up_1_subject: re: your MCP payments post and a thought
contacts.follow_up_1_content:
Hi Siva,

Following up from last week. One specific number that might be relevant to any retrieval work you're doing: on the Milvus vs Qdrant vs FAISS benchmark I ran at Silver Touch on real production data, the split was 95% / 85% / 45% MRR respectively. That kind of gap only shows up when you test on actual query distributions, not synthetic ones. Worth knowing if you're evaluating backends for any agentic pipeline.

Still interested in the engineering role if there's an opening.

Amit
amitgohel.dev
